With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. (I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above on the left is my fourteenth published book, and my first book on ritual magic that wasn't basically a rehash of what I'd learned as a Golden Dawn practitioner.  The Druidry Handbook, my ninth book (and one of my bestsellers), didn't talk about magic much -- its purpose was to help people make sense of Druid nature spirituality, which can be related to magic but doesn't have to be -- and I got a lot of questions from Druids, and people interested in Druidry, who wanted to know how to practice magic in a Druid context. This was my first answer. (We'll get to the others.) Looking back on this project, I'm pleased by it; it sets out a straightforward course of magical training and seems to work well for many students. It's still very much in print, and you can get a copy here if you live in the United States and at your favorite bookseller if you live elsewhere.

comments.title: Questions about bestsellers and full moons and bugs
comments.content:
Good evening dear fellow commentariat and may blessings enliven all who'd receive them. JMG, thank you again for hosting, inspiring, answering, informing. I'm glad you're awake, as I don't know if you click to approve comments in your sleep. :-)

1 It looks like most or maybe all of your books are about timeless topics. You've described in other Magic Mondays how you came across Druidry when it was obscure, and fading into even greater obscurity. Thus making a wide open opportunity for you to help others discover it. Unlike a topic such as "how to troubleshoot the latest version of Windows," things likely don't change much in one lifetime about Druidry spirituality. Does this translate into publishing longevity? It seems Druidry Handbook (with its invitingly mysterious cover) could be continuously reprinted year after year. What's your overall longest-term success with a book?

2 I have an astrology question. I see many people instruct to never start anything new from full moon to new moon. This seems extreme. Are we really to throw away half of all days when we could exercise initiative? Is this supposed to be only a minor factor between otherwise equally auspicious times?

3 I'll want to ask on Frugal Friday for pragmatic tips about dealing with bugs in an apartment. Here, I'll ask if there's a magical way to clarify that uninvited critters should stay out.

By the way, for those who might be confused, Christophe is a different person who also comments here. Our back to back comments here last week were two individual views, not my talking to myself with a typo.

Christopher from California
Mocha Amphibious Moose

replies.title: Re: Questions about bestsellers and full moons and bugs
replies.content:
1) Yes, very much so. I've written a number of books on more topical issues -- my peak oil books, for example -- and most of them are out of print or available only in ebook format at this point. By contrast, nearly all my occult books are all still going strong; some of them are on their third publisher, but others are still with the original press.

2) That's a mistaken approach to astrology. Astrology shows tendencies, not destinies -- thus during the period of the waning moon, it's harder to get something started and easier to finish something up or get rid of something. That doesn't mean you can't start something, just that it's a little more difficult, and if it's something that will be hard to start anyway, you might want to do it during the waxing moon instead.

3) Depends on what kind of magic you know how to do. The Picatrix includes spells for chasing off insects, but you have to be good at medieval astrological magic to use them.

replies.title: Re: Questions about bestsellers and full moons and bugs
replies.content:
Borax mixed with 2 parts to 1 part flour will also kill roaches. If you have to move into an apartment, mix up a truckload of this stuff. Apply IN A THIN LAYER under the sinks, in the cupboards, all the usual places, AND along all baseboards. (If you follow the natural human inclination to put down heaps, because roaches are so disgusting, the roaches will just shrug —“Gee, that’s funny, that big white hill wasn’t there yesterday”—and walk around it.). And NEVER USE BROWN PAPER GROCERY BAGS. Roaches lay eggs in the seams so the babies will have a nice nutritious glue meal when they hatch. If you are embarrassed to ask for plastic, take pillowcases to the store with you and use those.

Your borax solution should be reapplied weekly till you haven’t seen a roach for a month, then monthly after that. Good luck!

—Princess Cutekitten

comments.content:
Howdy,

I hope that everyone has had a productive and worthwhile start to the year and is looking forward to a longer-than-usual February.

To Share: Now I have really finished with my reviews of The Seed of Yggdrasill, as I put together an overview for anyone who doesn't want to slog through 12 blog posts to figure out whether you want to read the book or not: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_the_seed_of_yggdrasill_overall_rundown

To Ask: Nothing this week, just want to express my thanks to JMG and everyone here for the wisdom you share! This is always one of the highlights of my week, and has been a tremendous supplement to the more structured/directed parts of my occult study.

replies.title: Re: Overview of The Seed of Yggdrasill and Thanks
replies.content:
Hi Jeff, thanks for the offered blessings and may your week and all its studies be blessed.

Wouldn't 600 leaves make 1,200 pages?

You don't give any kind of explanation what the book is. I surmise that "A massive personal interpretation of the theology of ancient Norse myths" might be it. But this is only a guess from trying to identify, from m many remarks about leaves, that this might be something about a forest.

Mentioning but never defining "Eddic and skaldic poems," "the skalds," "wights, steads," "Kennings, heiti," "the whole Poetic Edda" in your review only mystifies someone not already familiar with Norse myths.

I get the impression that the book really needed an editor's hand. Alas, and with respect for all your slogging, I think this summary essay might, too.

An interesting comparison might be to how myths use multiple gods to represent aspects of one ultimate spiritual unity, between North mythology and other systems also said to do this, such as Hinduism.

I have the impression that I'd be 100% lost from page one of this book, and still missing the forest by the last leaf.

Christopher from California

replies.title: Re: Overview of The Seed of Yggdrasill and Thanks
replies.content:
Hi Christopher,

Thanks for sharing your thoughts and your blessings!

1) I use "leaves" instead of "pages," as I like to use as many words that come from Old English as I can in my writing, most of all when writing about heathenry (I don't go quite so far as "Anglish," but it's in the same vein: https://anglish.fandom.com/wiki/Main_leaf).

2) Okay, fair enough, I suppose I took for granted that the audience would have some idea of what the book was/was about, which may have been a mistake, so I might add a "What is This Book?" section. As for the terminology, it seems clunky to define all of those terms, but maybe I can throw in some wikipedia links or the like to make it easier for readers unfamiliar with them to look them up.

3) Ouch, but again, fair enough.

4) It certainly could be, but I am nowhere near knowledgeable enough to make that comparison in any detail. Kvilhaug mentions some of those comparisons in the book, but doesn't go very deeply into them.

5) Well, as I say in the review, this is definitely not a good "my first Norse myth" book. If you are interested in that, Hilda Ellis-Davidson's Gods and Myths of Northern Europe is quite good and pretty short, and E.O.G. Turville-Petre's Myth and Religion of the North is one of the most academically-respected of such works, but is a bit longer and can be pretty pricey (it's priced as a textbook that will be subsidized by a university).

Thanks again for your thoughts. As my old boss used to say "feedback is a gift,"
Jeff

comments.title: Change crystallization
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I've been looking at the way how are changes are sometimes forced onto systems and I'm seeing a certain pattern.

For the sake of example, let's imagine we are trying to make some state to become more democratic. We have a power to constantly talk about how democracy is good and beneficial, but we don't have a power or capacity to force everyone to behave the way we want (or maybe we don't know how exactly how people should behave on an individual level for society to become more democratic). So we bombard people minds with democratic propaganda, and for a time it works - the society indeed starts to slowly become more and more democratic as it absorbs the values. But then at some point as we push it, a sort of crystallization happens: part of society becomes ultrademocratic, while the remaining part becomes very distanced and often moves in the opposite direction of what we want. Further pressure towards democracy that we apply channels towards the ultrademocratic part of society, and never reaches the remaining part, and no amount of direct pressure could shift the balance between the part that is affected and not affected.

Don't take the example too seriously - this is just some example I lived through, and the society parts may be not just groups of people but spheres of life (e.g. someone can be pro-democracy on public, but against it in private life and so on). Similar things I observed pro/anti-vaccines, pro/anti-war and even on a lower scales in organizations or even individual life.

My question to you is -- is there anything you could recommend to read / learn on how to control the process -- e.g. prevent crystallization from happening if I don't want it to happen, accelerate it when I want it to happen, what to do when it already happened etc?

Thank you very much!
Oleg S

replies.title: Re: Change crystallization
replies.content: Dion Fortune talks about this in The Cosmic Doctrine, in symbolic terms. When you push too hard, too fast, and in too linear a direction, you inevitably get this kind of resistance. It requires a gentler, subtler, more patient, and less linear approach to move any complex system in a desired direction. You also have to be honest about your own intentions; if by "democracy" you mean a system in which your ideas are privileged over other people's, why, you can count on people who disagree with your ideas deciding to reject your agenda by rejecting the label you've given it.

replies.title: Re: Change crystallization
replies.content: Something I worked out that's sort of adjacent to being an alchemical analogy: If you want to mix a slightly hard-to-wet powder into water without the mixture getting lumpy, one thing to try is to start with the powder, and then introduce the water gradually and mix it until you have a thick slurry. That way, the slurry responds close to the same way to stirring as a lump of powder does, and so the slurry can deliver shearing force through to the lump and smear it out until it mixes. If you start with the water and dump the powder in, the powder forms clumps, and your stirring energy just gets dissipated in the liquid part of the mixture, without producing any shearing force to take the clumps apart.

comments.title: Evolution & the Nadir
comments.content:
Like so many others, we are going to introduce tablet-computers as the more or less sole working device in our school for all and everyone. It's a stupid decision that is going to cause a lot of damage to the children's education. The older students who are already using these devices know why and - as one of them said: You need to be very disciplined to really make use of these devices and not be carried away by all sorts of gimmickry that they provide. That is what's going to happen, of course. It's an open secret that where tablets are introduced, students will spent most of the time watching youtube or playing games during classes.

I think this is a fine example of "trying to evolve before crossing the nadir", that will not lead to evolution but dissolution. You need a solid foundation in manual and mental skills to be able to use a device that has the power to amplify your abilities if you use it correctly. If you haven't manifested these skills solidly enough and try to evolve your abilities anyway, you will ultimately loose more than you gain.

With that in mind and thinking about the role of the nadir in spiritual evolution - how can one be sure not to make the same mistake? And is it generally necessary to cross the nadir in all and everything you do or is it possible to loose a few abilities that hadn't been properly manifested during evolution? I guess if the latter is possible you will have evolved beings that have a different level of "skill" - for whatever that means on higher planes - those that have mastered certain abilities on the plane of matter and those who have not but ascended anyway?

Cheers,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Evolution & the Nadir
replies.content:
That's an interesting way of thinking of it! If your school was in the US I'd assume that the tablet salesman had bribed the school authorities -- that's how such things normally happen here -- but I don't know if that's true where you are. But yes, it's going to make your students less attentive and guarantee that they'll learn less.

As for your general question, crossing the nadir doesn't require a specific set of skills to be mastered -- it's simply a matter of coming to grips with the limitations and possibilities of the material plane, grappling with them, and being formed and transformed by the experience. No two evolved beings have exactly the same skills, btw, because no two of them have had exactly the same experiences in their lives in matter.

replies.title: Re: Evolution & the Nadir
replies.content:
Bribery is certainly an option - but I don't think that it was even necessary, unfortunately. In Germany we seem to have the habit of jumping on the rear end of a train where the front is already traveling in the opposite direction. But it's us, therefore success is guaranteed, of course.

I asked about the Nadir because Fortune writes about something where you seem to be only "halfway over" which seems to be undesirable. But I guess from your answer and my own feelings towards the matter that "following something to it's logical conclusion" is what it needed? Not to be satisfied with the 80% you can accomplish in 20% of the time but to go all the way until you know the work is done?

Greetings and thank you!
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Evolution & the Nadir
replies.content: Yes, very much so. Following through is essential.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Each house has two different sets of meanings. There's the meaning that it has by itself, and the meaning it has in relation to other houses. The 8th is the house of death, change, and magic -- that's the meaning it has by itself. It's also the house that follows the 7th, and so it functions as the 2nd house of the 7th -- thus it represents another person's money and property. That's true of every house, btw -- the 4th house, for example, represents the money and property of your siblings if you have any, since it's the 2nd of the 3rd; the 6th house represents the money and property of your children, and so on. Yes, it gets complex!

If the 8th house is coming up, I'd tend to think there's money or property involved in the dispute between the brothers, possibly (since the 8th is the natural house of Scorpio) money or property they aren't talking about...

comments.title: Conscious thought and the astral plane
comments.content:
Good morning, generous host:

The astral plane is described as a place we visit in our dreams, but I'm wondering where conscious, ordinary thought falls in the scheme of planes. It seems non-material, and not etheric either. Is ordinary thinking also part of the astral plane? What about our awareness of physical sensations? (I might be overthinking this, but I'm also genuinely interested and a bit confused.)

Thanks much.

replies.title: Re: Conscious thought and the astral plane
replies.content:
Spend a little while paying attention to your thoughts. Do they take the form of words? Images? Secondhand sensations, like memories? All these are phenomena of the astral plane. What makes thinking complex is that those words, images, or what have you are attempts to express a pattern of meaning, and meanings belong to the mental plane. So thinking at its best bridges the gap between those two planes.

As for your awareness of physical sensations, you become conscious of a sensation when the nerve impulse is converted to an astral perception.

replies.title: Re: Conscious thought and the astral plane
replies.content:
Your explanation helps me make sense of a lot of behavior I see around me. To put it bluntly, it seems like a lot of people just don't think. If that is the case - if their thoughts have no coherent, thought-out pattern of meaning and purpose tying them together - then a lot of human behavior is just reaction to whatever sorts of patterns happen to have astrally formed in their minds.

I'm not excusing myself here - there are parts of my life I seem to be reasonably coherent and self-aware, and there are other parts I don't have a handle on that seem like a formless, unaware and reactive mess.

Am I correctly understanding what you're saying here?

replies.title: Re: Conscious thought and the astral plane
replies.content: Exactly! One of the points of human incarnation is to learn to think. The fact that we need to learn how to do that implies, of course, that we don't start out with that capacity and have to develop it a step at a time. Yes, most human behavior is pure astral reaction; the only thing that differentiates it from animal behavior is that animals have instincts from their group souls to guide them, and we don't.

replies.title: Re: Conscious thought and the astral plane
replies.content:
That explains why the discipline of thoughtful meditation - I like the biblical phrase, pondering in your heart - is so very important.

That also explains why some sort of ascetic discipline and control of instinctive reactions and desires is such an important part of so many spiritual paths. It's less a moral matter and more a matter or training, education and awareness.

replies.title: Re: Conscious thought and the astral plane
replies.content: Once again, spot on.

replies.title: Re: Conscious thought and the astral plane
replies.content: Thank you both for the replies. Good food for thought, you might say.

replies.title: Re: Conscious thought and the astral plane
replies.content: Why do we lack a group soul?

replies.title: Re: Conscious thought and the astral plane
replies.content: So that we can exercise free will and receive the karmic feedback from our choices. Animals have only very limited capacity for free will because instinct, which is the voice of the group soul, controls so much of what they do. In order to become fully individual, conscious beings, we are cut loose from domination by a group soul when we become human, and we stumble around until we replace the guidance of the group soul with the capacity for reflective thought and conscious attunement with higher states of being.

replies.title: Re: Protecting a space during an open house.

replies.title: Re: Strengthening of the will

comments.title: St. Phanourios
comments.content:
Recently, I visited Greece and returned with a small icon of St. Panourios. I am not Orthodox but I was visiting a religious store to pick up some gifts and souvenir items.

The icon for St. Panourios appealed to me and while I was at the register the nice ladies there started telling me about the items I purchased. When I admitted I didn't know who the saint was, one of them looked amazed that I could go through life and not know this saint!!. "You don't know??".

St. Panourios is the saint of lost items. This belief started when his icon was discovered intact after a monastery was looted by raiders. There is also a tradition of baking a cake associated with him.

Link below for full details.

When I travel I hide certain items at home, just in case and take notes on where I put them. Sometimes I hide them two well. There was one item I didn't take a note for, I was looking around but later, when I unpacked the icon, I placed it right next to where I found the item. Later, I hung the icon on a book shelf and I found records and paperwork that I had long forgotten about.

As I'm not Orthodox I'm not sure how they go about asking the saint and giving thanks to the saint. Anyone one that has insight into that I would appreciate it. I'll research but always best to hear from those that know.

Many Greeks take their icons seriously and when one visits a church, you'll see the faithful give the icons two kisses, just like kissing someone on both cheeks.

https://orthodoxwiki.org/Phanourios

replies.title: Re: St. Phanourios
replies.content:
The name is Phanourios, starts with an "F" sound. Generally the way that works is, you need to find something, you can't find it (like, I have done this when my toddler lost my car keys. Had been missing for days. Really really needed them back), so you say a prayer to St. Phanourios. He is very helpful. After you find the lost item, you bake a Phanouropita ("Phanourios" + "cake/bread" but in Greek bread is "pita") and you take it to coffee hour at church and share it with everybody. This is how you say thank you. If you are not Orthodox, I suppose you could bake the Phanouropita and... figure out someone else to share it with, out of gratitude to the saint? It is not hard to find recipes online. There's different ways to bake it but it's basically some variation of a spice cake.

There is some fun trivia about St. Phanourios. There's not a lot of evidence that he actually existed, and it's possible the found icon was an icon of St. George that somebody mis-read. Nobody really knows. But if it was really St. George, he seems happy to help you find your lost things, even under a pseudonym ;)

comments.title: Chronic Illness and Karma
comments.content:
Thank you for your time, as always.

Is there anything you happen to know about the karmic dimensions of chronic illness? I've been fighting a debilitating medical condition since I was a teenager, one that involves chronic pain that many sufferers describe as "excruciating". The mainstream medical treatments available are generally both very painful and not very effective either. I am extremely fortunate to have discovered that being extremely disciplined in my diet allows me to control my illness to an extent, which has afforded me the ability to live a more or less normal life, with the occasional flare up here and there. However, lately, I've been worried that perhaps this illness will follow me into subsequent lives, and am wondering if there's anything else I should be doing to ensure that the karma around this is cleared up properly? Is there anything else I should pay attention to besides simply managing and living with it? Or is it possible that I am thinking about this in entirely the wrong way?

Any insight you could provide would be very much appreciated, but I understand if this is out of your wheelhouse.

replies.title: Re: Chronic Illness and Karma
replies.content: This is something a lot of occult writings mention. What you're doing -- bearing up and living the best possible life despite a difficult medical condition -- is burning off karma, not storing up more of it. The fact that you've already found a way to mitigate the condition through the conscious action of disciplined control of your diet shows that you've already gotten the upper hand over the karmic burden, and it should be over and done with by the end of this incarnation. It's possible that you may find an opportunity to help other people, possibly by sharing your experience with others who have the same illness; that would be worth doing, as it will create positive karma to tip the scale the right way. comments.title: Physical/psychological cross-effects (affirmation)
comments.content:
I have never been athletic. About one year ago, to improve my posture for health reasons, I booked a session with a personal trainer. He recommended a set of exercises to maintain my abdominal muscles tensed while performing other movements. I have followed these exercises quite regularly 1-2x a week (plus other exercises) and felt a continuous improvement in my posture during everyday activities like walking in the street and lately even while standing or sitting at home.

Now I need to add that I have never had a lot of self-confidence with women, but by a stroke of luck, I married a wonderful and also very attractive woman. I think I have been giving her a lot of emotional and also career support, but for many years, since our only child was born, we have had much less intimacy than I would wish.

What I hadn't expected from my workout was a considerable boost to my self-esteem from walking with a better posture. I also seem to stand out more to strangers. Some weeks ago, I decided to try and further potentialize the effect. However, I do want to avoid certain complications. The affirmation I chose to repeat to myself while walking in the street or while in places with other people is: "I am desirable, and I only desire my wife".

The effect of that affirmation on myself is strong, I feel almost giddy at times, as if I were seven feet tall, and at the same time, it is much easier to control myself so as not to gaze at unknown women. On the other hand, I haven't felt any change in my wife's behavior towards me.

Do you think this kind of affirmation is healthy?

Umber Seedy Gecko

replies.title: Re: Physical/psychological cross-effects (affirmation)
replies.content: On reflection, I'm going to say a little more about this, even though it gets into controversial territory. One of the differences between men and women is that for many women, the sex drive is much more tightly linked to the reproductive drive than in most men. Once a woman has had the children she intends to have, fairly often a switch gets flipped and she loses interest in sex. This isn't a conscious thing; it happens down at the biological levels of the self, and it isn't negotiable. It just happens. If that's what has taken place in your marriage, you're just going to have to live with it.

replies.title: Re: Physical/psychological cross-effects (affirmation)

replies.title: Re: Physical/psychological cross-effects (affirmation)

comments.title: Compatibility between FHR, AODA and GSF
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

Thank you for hosting these Magic Mondays.

I have a question about the extent of the compatibility between the Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose, the Ancient Order of Druids in America and the Golden Section Fellowship.

I discovered these three Traditions over the past few years and developed a keen interest in them. The fact that they were said to be mutually compatible certainly increased their appeal. However, as I didn’t quite know where to start, I decided to go for the FHR. Indeed, I happen to have a background of many years in the Golden Dawn style of magic, so the FHR, which has Qabalah (i.e. the Tree of Life) as a foundation and, above all, allows the LBRP as basic protective ritual, was an obvious choice. To be frank, I had a go at the Sphere of Protection, as I found it really appealing, but much to my regret I never really managed to get the hang of it. I guess that after so many years practicing the LBRP I had a hard time adjusting to the SoP and its specificities.

As long as I was involved with FHR work exclusively, however, that was just fine. But now that I’m expanding my horizon towards the GSF I realize that practicing the LBRP rather than the SoP as basic protective ritual might severely limit my options, e.g. as regards the invocation of the Sun and Earth currents and the formulation of the Moon current. (This also applies to the AODA, btw.) So I feel sort of stuck: the SoP seems to be indispensable, after all, but some of its “technicalities” still elude me and I'm not sure there is anything I can do about it.

Do you have any suggestions? Is there a way to do the work of the Three Currents in another way? Or are there simply no alternatives?

Thank you for reading this message and for any insights you may offer, that will be very appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Compatibility between FHR, AODA and GSF
replies.content:
If you're going to do the GSF or AODA work, you need to take up the SoP. Trust me, I know the feeling of "not getting it"! When John Gilbert first taught me the SoP I didn't like it at all -- I had twenty years of Golden Dawn practice under my belt at that time, btw. It took patience and relentless practice before I realized that it's not that I wasn't getting it -- it's that it's a different kind of ritual: subtler, gentler, and requiring time and practice for its maturation.

My advice to you is that you should grit your teeth, set aside the LBRP, and do the SoP daily even though it feels like you're not getting it. Over time, you'll begin to get a sense of what it does and how it works.

comments.title: Astral chatter and Mental silence
comments.content:
Greetings to our generous host and our fine commentariat!

Last week Nachtgurke (love the name!) wrote in a post about experiencing a sudden cessation of mental chatter during the SOP, I think it was, and you commented that this was the result of consciousness rising momentarily out of the Astral plane and into the Mental plane.

This makes me remember some observations I made of my dear mother during the last months of her life, suffering from Alzheimer's. She spent the last months in a state of withdrawal of ordinary consciousness, not unconscious, exactly--she could be fed and swallow her food for example, and occasionally responded by head or hand movements to what was going on around her, but her eyes were closed and she paid almost no attention to anything.

Except she had a constant, and I mean constant, stream of murmuring going on. The kind of thing most of us would hear if we orally expressed the constant mental chatter going on in our heads: words and sentences, but context and referents are lacking for those who would be listening; it's just a stream of mysterious chatter. I often tried to decipher what she was saying; I wanted to respond to her so she might feel, on some level, that she was being heard and attended to, but it usually made very little sense.

There are certainly physiological reasons for this behavior, and there are some interesting implications for my Heathen soul lore studies, too. But when I read the discussion with Nachtgurke last week, it brought to mind what you write in the Occult Philosophy Workbook about experiences after death in the Astral plane, and you also have talked about picking up random astral information / signals / influences when scrying and similar kinds of attunements.

These observations about my mother seem to reinforce those insights; she seems to have been (a) serving as an 'astral antenna,' so to speak (which can help explain her bouts of anxiety and confusion), and (b) kind of previewing her post-death experience when entering the astral realm. Based on my brief, deliberate 'visits' with her after her death, she seem to have settled in nicely to the current phase of her afterlife, surrounded by kin and friends and being very sociable, which could also explain her constant need to talk before her death. She was the hub of our extended family, the connection-point who kept everyone together and in touch, everyone's favorite relative.

The other function of her mental chatter, I believe, was that she had an extreme resistance to death. She did not believe in an afterlife, and I think she was holding death--or rather, her feared entry into nonexistence--at bay as long as she could by establishing the reality of her own existence through her power of speech. The ironic thing was that as she neared death, her own parents were so very present in spirit with her; my husband and I could both sense them. She was so busy with her stream of talk that she could not hear the loving and reassuring words of her parents.

Well, I don't mean to ramble. This is kind of an observation of the existential power of human speech--power which can move us in amazing new directions, and power which can hold us back from deeper awareness, too. And this is connected, I think, with the ways that we function on the astral versus the mental realm. It's all very interesting to think about!

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Astral chatter and Mental silence
replies.content: Thank you for this! I've seen the same thing in dying people -- I used to work, many years ago, in nursing homes, and got very familiar with the dying process -- and your thesis certainly fits what I saw.

comments.title: respect
comments.content:
I'm looking for a positive affirmation to help stop others to treat me like crap and stop treating me like a door mat or punching bag. Getting pretty sabotaged at work lately. Maybe it's because the ship is sinking but there's a reason I'm getting pushed out the door first, and I'd like to take the lesson about the dangers of trying to please people and being too nice to whatever endeavors come next.

"I deserve respect" sounds kind of entitled

"I earn respect and demand people show it to me." Not sure I like the demand part.

"I set limits and do not tolerate disrespect?"

replies.title: Re: respect
replies.content:
Okay, all three of these have problems. Saying that you deserve respect doesn't mean you will get it -- most of us deserve things we don't get. The same thing is true of "demand" -- doesn't mean anyone will consent to your demand. As for "do not tolerate," remember that the subconscious doesn't hear words like "no" and "not".

All this focuses directly or indirectly on what you don't want, not what you do. An affirmation I used with very good results handles the issue differently: "I am the master." Try that on for size and see how it feels.

replies.title: Re: respect
replies.content: Wow. Love it. Thanks!

comments.title: Astrology and Hexagrams
comments.content:
Hi! Two questions:

1: a dear friend, and I are thinking of beginning a project on the first day of Aries. I suggested this date because I did the chart and the aspect seemed good, but also I know that that is the beginning of the astrological year. My understanding is that the astrological new year is a good time for new beginnings and the start of new projects and adventures. Is that correct? Or is there something I’m missing?

2: How necessary is it to do Greater Hexagram consecration of talismans on the day of its correspondence? Can one create a talisman of Venus on a Wednesday, for example?

As always, many thanks!

replies.title: Re: Astrology and Hexagrams
replies.content:
1) Yes, but it can also be a very rough day to begin things. If the chart's good, by all means -- but the energies of the new year can be tumultuous, so you'll want to brace for turbulence at the beginning.

2) The hexagram rituals can be done at any time. That's one of the secrets of Golden Dawn magic -- it accesses the levels of being above the astrological realm, and so is independent of time. You can give a little extra boost by also using planetary days and hours, or electing a chart, but you don't have to.

comments.title: AI image generation
comments.content:
I believe I have found a new emanating of the void in our lives - AI image generation. If this seems like the phrase in the cos doc that describes the void - "where men can be as gods" in the absence of constraints and limits, it reflects that. "So it is that all evolving force, whether in the universe or in the individual, having penetrated to fundamentals, looks across the great gulf fixed by Cosmic Law and sees the simulacra of its hopeless dreams promising fulfillment" -- that's how Dion Fortune describes it in The Cosmic Doctrine.

replies.title: Re: AI image generation
replies.content: I'm guessing the thing that does the reflection is the penumbra? And also guessing this is the point you tell me to meditate on that ;)

replies.title: Re: AI image generation
replies.content: Correct on both counts!

replies.title: Re: AI image generation
replies.content:
It's not the ultimate void, containing nothing but infinite creative potential. Everything a Large Model AI generates is only a pastiche of elements from the material provided to its training phase. It doesn't come up with anything completely original, absolutely unprecedented, beyond recombined snippets of what was previously shown to it.

Depending on the qualities of the original art and the ingenuity of the prompt, this copy and paste job could result in a shuffling of some deep archetypal associations, the way a tornado can rip out some pieces of property and fling them around.

But a Large Model AI will never make something completely unlike the training material, something impossible to have predicted from what's been done before, the true mark of genius masterpieces of human creativity. Everything dumped in a pile by the tornado's passing was already present when it arrived.

Christopher from California

replies.title: Re: AI image generation
replies.content: The thing that fascinates me about AI imagery is precisely that it seems to be less than the sum of its ingredients. It's not just that it's a pastiche of existing imagery. It's a pastiche around an emptiness. The tornado doesn't just fling things around, it takes something away with it that ois never found again.
